name: Larry Clinton
abstract: Larry Clinton (August 17, 1909  –  May 2, 1985) was an American musician, best known as a trumpeter who became a prominent American bandleader and arranger. His jazz and pop standards were "The Dipsy Doodle" (1937), "My Reverie" (1938), and "Heart and Soul" (1938).

about_quotes.speaker: Benny Golson
about_quotes.evidence: Who knows about Larry Clinton? Larry Clinton was big when I was coming up as a kid in the ’30s. You mention Larry Clinton – who? But if you mention Duke Ellington, comes from the same period, yes, everybody knows.
about_quotes.doc_title: Benny Golson — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Avakian George
about_quotes.evidence: The Basie band was more popular than Lunceford, but even Basie, I think, as far as the public was concerned, was way behind these other bands, Larry Clinton and so on and so on.
about_quotes.doc_title: Avakian George — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Jonah Jones
about_quotes.evidence: Sammy told me they had about 100 bands, Ina Ray Hutton, Larry Clinton, Duke and Cab and a whole host of bands.

about_quotes.speaker: Mandel Johnny
about_quotes.evidence: I heard him at the Park Central, I heard Larry Clinton at the Park Central I remember at the time.
about_quotes.doc_title: Mandel Johnny — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Chauncey Morehouse
about_quotes.evidence: Do you remember substituting for Larry Clinton's drummer on 'The Campbells Are Swinging'.

about_quotes.speaker: Big Chielf Moore
about_quotes.evidence: wishing I could be in the band, you know, I'm dreaming, fantasizing.
